choices: Membership-inference and model-inversion attacks can extract training-data information from deployed models, especially with rare/unique examples; mitigations: differential privacy during training, output filtering / Bedrock Guardrails for sensitive content, rate limiting + monitoring suspicious query patterns, redact PII from training data pre-training, regular red-team testing
Models never leak training data
Encryption alone prevents all leakage
There is nothing to mitigate
questions.explanation:
Trained model weights can encode training data: research shows LLMs reproduce training-set strings, image models reconstruct training images. Membership inference (was X in training?) and model inversion (recover training data) attacks are real for some scenarios. Mitigations: (1) Differential privacy during training (adds calibrated noise, bounds individual contribution). (2) Pre-training data filtering (remove PII, copyrighted content, sensitive examples). (3) Output filtering — for LLMs, Bedrock Guardrails blocks PII patterns. (4) Rate limiting + suspicious-query monitoring (extraction attacks need many queries). (5) Red-team testing — actively try to extract data and patch findings. (6) For small or sensitive datasets, consider not training a deployed model at all and using RAG instead (data stays in retrieval, not weights).

Options B, C, D are wrong. Encryption protects data at rest/transit, not from being learned by the model. The trap: candidates may not consider model-as-attack-surface.
questions.pattern: Privacy-preserving ML training: differential privacy (add noise during training; bounded individual influence). Federated learning (train on distributed data without centralizing). Secure multi-party computation, homomorphic encryption (compute on encrypted data — slow). Data minimization (less personal data). Synthetic data generation. RAG over training when knowledge can stay external.

questions.scenario: A team needs to demonstrate compliance for a regulated workload (HIPAA, PCI, FedRAMP). They need centralized evidence collection across AWS services.
questions.question: Which AWS service is purpose-built for AUTOMATED COMPLIANCE EVIDENCE COLLECTION?
choices: AWS Audit Manager — pre-built frameworks (HIPAA, PCI DSS, SOC 2, FedRAMP, GDPR, ISO 27001, etc.); automatically collects evidence from CloudTrail, Config, Security Hub, IAM Access Analyzer, etc.; produces audit-ready assessment reports
CloudTrail alone is the report
Manually screenshot the console
Compliance is automatic in AWS
questions.explanation:
AWS Audit Manager is the dedicated compliance evidence service. Pre-built frameworks (HIPAA, PCI DSS, SOC 2, FedRAMP, GDPR, ISO 27001, NIST, AWS Operational Best Practices, and many more) translate regulatory controls into automated checks against AWS resources. Sources: CloudTrail (API audit), Config (resource compliance), Security Hub (security findings), IAM Access Analyzer (permission analysis), CloudWatch (metrics). Generates assessment reports with evidence trail. Customers still own customer-side controls and configuration; Audit Manager automates the collection of cloud-side evidence.

Option B is too narrow. Option C doesn't scale. Option D is wrong (compliance is shared responsibility — AWS provides certifications + tools; customer configures + audits). The trap: candidates may know individual services (CloudTrail, Config, Security Hub) but not the orchestration service.
questions.pattern: Compliance stack: AWS Artifact (download AWS attestations: SOC, ISO, etc.). Audit Manager (collect customer-side evidence). Config + Security Hub + GuardDuty (monitor + alert). CloudTrail (audit log). Trusted Advisor (best-practice checks). For ML specifically: SageMaker Model Cards, Bedrock invocation logging, Model Registry approvals — all evidence sources.

questions.scenario: A team uses SageMaker Feature Store. They want a feature 'avg_purchase_30d' computed as a 30-day rolling average. The feature must be computed identically for both training (historical, point-in-time) and serving (real-time per-user lookup).
questions.question: Which Feature Store capabilities ensure consistency?
choices: Single feature definition shared between online (DynamoDB-backed, low-latency) and offline (S3 Parquet) stores; ingestion is dual-written so the online store has the latest value and the offline store has all history with event-time timestamps; training uses point-in-time queries against the offline store to avoid leakage; same feature group + name + definition used at serving time
Compute features twice independently for training and serving
Use online store only — discard history
Use offline store only — slow lookups are fine
questions.explanation:
SageMaker Feature Store has dual-store architecture: online store (DynamoDB-backed, single-digit ms latency, latest value per record ID) and offline store (S3 Parquet, all history with event-time, queryable via Athena/Spark for training). Ingestion writes to both. Training-set construction uses point-in-time queries (asof joins) against the offline store, ensuring features reflect what was known at the labeled event time — preventing leakage. Same feature group definition used everywhere — eliminates training-serving skew at the feature level. For real-time inference, GetRecord against the online store returns the current feature values.

Option B causes training-serving skew. Options C, D break either training or serving. The trap: candidates may not realize the dual-store + point-in-time design is the whole point.
questions.pattern: Feature Store discipline: one definition, dual stores, point-in-time queries for training, GetRecord for serving. Streaming ingestion via PutRecord (or Kinesis Streams + Lambda for buffered). Time-to-Live (TTL) for online store records. Lineage tracking links features → models. For new features: backfill into offline store from historical data, then keep online store updated.

questions.scenario: A team has petabytes of raw and transformed data. They want a managed catalog for ML practitioners to discover datasets, view schemas and lineage, request access, and govern usage — across multiple AWS accounts.
questions.question: Which AWS service is purpose-built for ENTERPRISE DATA GOVERNANCE and discovery?
choices: Amazon DataZone — federated data catalog with discovery UI, business glossary, subscription-based access requests with approval workflows, lineage tracking, multi-account/region support; integrates with Glue Data Catalog, Lake Formation, Redshift, S3
S3 Inventory reports
CloudWatch dashboards
Trusted Advisor
questions.explanation:
Amazon DataZone is the AWS data-governance and catalog service: discovery (search by business terms, tags), business glossary (define domain vocabulary), subscriptions (data consumers request access; producers approve), lineage (where did this dataset come from, where does it go), multi-account federation, integrates with Glue, Lake Formation, Redshift. Provides a managed UI and APIs. Designed for enterprise scenarios where data scientists need to find data without trawling raw S3 paths or asking around. SageMaker Catalog (built on DataZone) extends this to ML assets.

Options B, C, D address operational topics, not data governance. The trap: candidates may try to compose Glue + manual processes, missing that DataZone is the integrated solution.
questions.pattern: Data governance stack: Glue Data Catalog (technical metadata). Lake Formation (fine-grained access). DataZone (business catalog, discovery, subscriptions). Macie (PII discovery). Glue Data Quality (validation). SageMaker Catalog (ML asset extension of DataZone). Combine for end-to-end: data discovery → access request → approval → enforced permissions → quality checks → lineage.

questions.scenario: A SageMaker training job repeatedly downloads 500 GB of data from S3 to the training instance. The team trains often (sometimes 10x/day) — re-downloading every time wastes time and S3 transfer.
questions.question: Which SageMaker / AWS feature avoids repeated S3 downloads?
choices: FSx for Lustre file system linked to the S3 bucket — sub-millisecond access, hundreds of GB/s throughput, persistent across training jobs; alternative: SageMaker FastFile mode (lazy stream from S3 instead of full download); or SageMaker training warm pools (keeps instance + downloaded data hot for next run)
Run training without data
Re-download every time without caching
Email data to the instance
questions.explanation:
Multiple optimizations for repeated training data access: (1) **FSx for Lustre** — POSIX file system, persistent, sub-ms latency, hundreds of GB/s aggregate, can be linked to S3 (lazy-load on first access, persistent thereafter). Best for repeated training and very large datasets. (2) **SageMaker FastFile mode** — streams data lazily from S3 (no full download upfront), good for one-shot training. (3) **SageMaker warm pools** — keep training instance up to 1 hour after job completes; if next job uses same instance type/config, starts faster with data still hot in EBS. (4) **Pipe mode** (older) — streaming for compatible algorithms. (5) **S3 Express One Zone** — ultra-low-latency single-AZ S3 for hot reads.

Options B, C, D are wrong/wasteful. The trap: candidates may not connect storage tier choice with training cost.
questions.pattern: Training-data delivery: File mode (download all). FastFile (lazy-stream from S3). Pipe (streaming, supported algorithms only). FSx for Lustre (linked S3, persistent, fastest). S3 Express One Zone (hot reads). Warm pools (reuse instance + data). Choose by data size, repetition rate, sensitivity to startup latency, cost.

questions.scenario: A team is building a tabular ML model. They want fast EDA and visualization, with the ability to apply transforms (impute, encode, scale, feature crosses) using a no-code UI before exporting clean data for training.
questions.question: Which SageMaker tool fits?
choices: SageMaker Data Wrangler — visual data prep in SageMaker Studio, 300+ built-in transforms, quick model insights (target leakage detection, multicollinearity, predictive power), exports flow to a SageMaker Processing job (or notebook code) for production
SageMaker Model Monitor
SageMaker Neo
SageMaker Edge Manager
questions.explanation:
SageMaker Data Wrangler is the visual data-prep tool inside SageMaker Studio. Connects to S3, Athena, Redshift, Snowflake, Databricks, etc. Provides 300+ transforms (imputation, encoding, scaling, feature crosses, custom Pandas/PySpark/SQL). Visualizations: histograms, scatter, correlation, target leakage analysis, quick-model evaluation. Once your flow is ready, export to: a SageMaker Processing job (production), a notebook (Python code), Feature Store, or Pipelines step. Reduces time-to-insight for tabular EDA dramatically.

Options B, C, D are unrelated SageMaker components. The trap: candidates may not know Data Wrangler exists and write all data prep manually in notebooks.
questions.pattern: EDA + data prep on AWS: Data Wrangler (visual, no/low-code). SageMaker Studio notebooks with pandas/Polars/Dask (code-first). Glue Studio (visual ETL). EMR Studio (notebook on EMR). For production, always export to a Processing job or Pipeline step — don't rely on interactive flow at runtime.

questions.scenario: A team needs to ensure that their training pipeline detects schema drift in source data — e.g., a new column appears, a column type changes, or an expected column disappears.
questions.question: Which approach catches SCHEMA DRIFT before it breaks training?
choices: Maintain a schema definition (Glue Schema Registry, JSON Schema, Avro), validate incoming data against it (Glue Data Quality rules, Deequ, Great Expectations), fail fast with clear errors when drift detected, version schemas and coordinate evolution (backward/forward compatibility)
Trust the source pipeline blindly
Re-train and hope it works
Disable validation to keep pipelines flowing
questions.explanation:
Schema validation is a discipline distinct from value-distribution monitoring. Approaches: (1) **Schema registry** — Glue Schema Registry, Confluent Schema Registry — enforce Avro/Protobuf/JSON Schema. (2) **Validation step** — Glue Data Quality with DQDL rules, Deequ, Great Expectations. Fail fast: bad pipeline run is better than silently corrupting downstream. (3) **Schema evolution** — define backward (new readers handle old data) and forward (old readers handle new data) compatibility rules, version schemas, coordinate with producers. (4) **Alerting** — drift triggers ops alarm + auto-pauses pipelines.

Options B, C, D are anti-patterns. The trap: candidates focus on value drift (Model Monitor) and miss the simpler schema-level guarantees.
questions.pattern: Schema-level vs distribution-level monitoring: schema (column existence, types, basic constraints — catches structural drift). Distribution (value statistics, KL divergence — catches subtle drift). Both needed. Tools: Glue Schema Registry, Glue Data Quality, Deequ, Great Expectations, Model Monitor. Fail-fast on schema drift; alert-and-investigate on distribution drift.

questions.scenario: A team's training data has time-correlated structure (e.g., daily user behavior). They split randomly 80/20 and observe great test accuracy — but in production, accuracy is much lower.
questions.question: Why is the offline metric misleading, and what split should they use?
choices: Random splitting on time-correlated data leaks future information into training (e.g., user behavior on Tuesday helps predict Monday); use time-based splits (train on past, test on future) and time-series cross-validation (rolling window or expanding window) to honestly estimate forward-prediction accuracy
Random split is always best
Use 99/1 split to be safe
Don't split at all
questions.explanation:
Random splits violate temporal independence when data has time structure. Adjacent points in time tend to be similar, so randomly placing some in train and others in test creates correlated train/test pairs — overestimates generalization. Production sees only future data, never randomly-mixed past. Correct: chronological split (train on Jan-Oct, validate on Nov, test on Dec). For more robust estimates: time-series cross-validation — rolling-window (fixed train window slides forward) or expanding-window (training set grows). For time-aware feature engineering: never use future information.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates apply random splits as a default without considering data structure.
questions.pattern: Splitting strategies by data type: i.i.d. data ⇒ random split is fine. Time-series ⇒ chronological split, time-series CV. Grouped data (multiple records per user/customer) ⇒ group-aware split (all of a user's records in same fold). Imbalanced classes ⇒ stratified split. Always: validate that your offline metric correlates with production performance over time.

questions.scenario: A team's training data contains personal information (names, addresses, payment data) that should not be visible to data scientists. They need to make data analyzable while protecting identities.
questions.question: Which combination of techniques applies?
choices: Tokenization (replace identifiers with reversible tokens), masking (irreversible: hash, redaction), pseudonymization (consistent fake IDs preserving join structure), differential privacy for aggregate releases, Macie for PII discovery in S3, Lake Formation column-level access for need-to-know views, synthetic data generation for shareable analysis sets
Show all data to all analysts
Delete all sensitive columns making analysis impossible
Encrypt only — encryption alone allows full access at runtime
questions.explanation:
Privacy-preserving analytics layered approach: (1) **Tokenization** — reversible mapping (token → real value, key in vault), preserves referential integrity for joins. (2) **Masking** — irreversible (hash, redact, partial mask like XXX-XX-1234). (3) **Pseudonymization** — replace IDs with consistent fake IDs (same person → same pseudonym across datasets). (4) **Differential privacy** — for releases of aggregate statistics, add calibrated noise to bound individual contribution. (5) **Discovery** — Macie scans S3 for PII automatically. (6) **Access control** — Lake Formation column-level access; analysts see masked views, privileged users see real values. (7) **Synthetic data** — generated with similar statistics but no real records.

Options B, C, D miss nuance. The trap: candidates conflate encryption (at rest, transparent at runtime) with access control (who can see what at runtime).
questions.pattern: Privacy techniques by use case: tokenization (need to join, not analyze content). Masking (analyze structure but not values). Pseudonymization (linkable across datasets without identity). Differential privacy (aggregate releases). Synthetic data (broad sharing). Encryption (at-rest/in-transit, separate from access control). Combine: Macie discovers, Glue/Lambda transforms, Lake Formation controls access.

questions.scenario: A team's training data has 50 features. They suspect many are redundant or unimportant. They want to identify the most informative features.
questions.question: Which feature-selection approaches are MOST APPROPRIATE?
choices: Combine: filter methods (correlation, mutual information, ANOVA F-test — fast, model-agnostic), wrapper methods (recursive feature elimination, sequential selection — model-aware, slower), embedded methods (Lasso/L1 regularization, tree feature importance, SHAP values — built-in to model training); cross-validate to avoid overfitting feature selection itself
Just keep all features regardless
Drop features randomly
Use only one feature
questions.explanation:
Feature selection has three families: (1) **Filter** — independent of model, fast: correlation with target, mutual information, ANOVA F-test, chi-squared. Use for initial pruning. (2) **Wrapper** — uses a specific model, slower: recursive feature elimination (RFE), sequential forward/backward selection. Better-quality but expensive. (3) **Embedded** — selection happens during model training: Lasso/L1 zeros out coefficients of irrelevant features; tree-based feature importance (XGBoost, RF); SHAP values. (4) Avoid selection-then-evaluate leakage: select features inside cross-validation folds, not on the full data. SageMaker Clarify provides SHAP-based feature importance.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may know one method but not when to use which.
questions.pattern: Feature selection decision: many features + need fast initial pruning ⇒ filter (correlation, mutual info). Want best subset for specific model ⇒ wrapper (RFE, forward/backward). Linear models with sparsity ⇒ Lasso/L1. Tree-based ⇒ feature importance + SHAP. Always cross-validate properly. Beware: feature selection can leak signal if done outside CV folds.

questions.scenario: A team's image classification training fails because images come in many sizes (224x224, 512x512, 1024x768, etc.) and the model expects a fixed input size.
questions.question: Which image preprocessing standardization steps are MOST APPROPRIATE?
choices: Resize all images to model's expected size (e.g., 224x224 for ImageNet-pretrained); maintain aspect ratio with padding or center-crop; normalize pixel values (mean/std from ImageNet for transfer learning, or your own dataset); convert color space to model expectation (typically RGB); apply train-only data augmentation (random crop, flip, color jitter, MixUp); use Pillow/OpenCV/TF-image for transforms
Train on raw images of arbitrary size
Convert all images to text
Drop images that don't match the size
questions.explanation:
Standard image preprocessing for CNNs: (1) **Resize** to model's input size (often 224x224 or 384x384). (2) **Aspect-ratio handling** — pad to square (preserves content) or center-crop (loses content but no distortion); choose based on what matters. (3) **Normalize** pixel values to mean=0, std=1 — typically using ImageNet stats `[0.485, 0.456, 0.406]` mean and `[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]` std for transfer learning, or your dataset's stats for from-scratch. (4) **Color space** — usually RGB; some models expect BGR (e.g., older OpenCV-based models). (5) **Train-only augmentation** — random crop, horizontal flip, color jitter, MixUp/CutMix; never apply at validation/test. (6) **Implementation** — TF data pipelines, PyTorch transforms, Albumentations for fast augmentation.

Option B will cause shape mismatches. Option C is nonsense. Option D loses data. The trap: candidates may forget normalization or apply augmentation at inference.
questions.pattern: Image preprocessing pipeline: resize → normalize → augment (train-only) → batch. Vision Transformer variants may use larger inputs (384, 512) or patch tokens. For production: ensure exact same preprocessing at inference as training; small differences cause significant accuracy drop. Common pitfalls: BGR vs RGB, normalize before vs after, augmentation leaking into test.

questions.scenario: A team's text dataset is in English, French, German, Japanese, and Chinese. They need a tokenizer that handles all languages and unknown words gracefully for an LLM fine-tuning task.
questions.question: Which tokenization approach is MOST APPROPRIATE?
choices: Subword tokenization (Byte-Pair Encoding, WordPiece, SentencePiece, Unigram) — splits words into subword units, handles unknown words by composition, works across languages, used by virtually all modern LLMs; for production, use the SAME tokenizer the base model was trained with
Whitespace splitting only — works for everything
Character-level tokenization always
Drop non-English text
questions.explanation:
Modern multi-language NLP uses subword tokenization: (1) **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)** — used by GPT family, Llama, Claude. (2) **WordPiece** — used by BERT. (3) **SentencePiece** — language-agnostic, handles whitespace as a token (good for Asian languages without spaces). (4) **Unigram language model** — alternative used by some models (T5, XLNet). All split rare words into more frequent subword pieces, eliminating the OOV (out-of-vocabulary) problem. Critically: when fine-tuning, USE THE SAME TOKENIZER as the base model — vocab IDs must match.

Option B fails for Japanese/Chinese (no spaces). Option C balloons sequence length and loses higher-level semantics. Option D abandons multilingual capability. The trap: candidates may default to whitespace tokenization without considering Asian languages.
questions.pattern: NLP tokenization: subword (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece, Unigram) — modern default. Whitespace + lowercase — only for very simple English-only tasks. Character — useful for some morphologically rich languages, but expensive. Always: pair model with its tokenizer; vocab IDs are not transferable across models. Hugging Face `AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained()` ensures correct pairing.

questions.scenario: A team's training data labels contain noise — some rows have wrong labels (annotator error). They want to train a robust model.
questions.question: Which approaches are MOST APPROPRIATE for handling noisy labels?
choices: Identify noisy labels (cross-validation predictions, confident learning, cleanlab library), relabel/remove suspected errors with a second annotator, use loss functions robust to label noise (label smoothing, focal loss for imbalance + noise, generalized cross-entropy), train large model with regularization (it's harder for noise to dominate), iterative cleaning + retraining
Trust all labels regardless of evidence to the contrary
Discard the entire dataset
Use random labels — they're equivalent
questions.explanation:
Label-noise handling techniques: (1) **Detection** — train initial model with cross-validation, examine predictions where the model is highly confident but disagrees with the label (cleanlab/confident learning algorithms). (2) **Relabeling** — route suspect labels to a second annotator for review, fix systematic errors. (3) **Robust loss functions** — label smoothing (don't train to extreme one-hot, soften to e.g. 0.9), generalized cross-entropy (less sensitive to wrong labels), bootstrapping. (4) **Regularization** — large models with dropout/weight decay are more resistant to fitting noise. (5) **Iterative** — train, find errors, fix, retrain. (6) **Active learning** — use model uncertainty to direct labeling effort to most-impactful examples.

Options B, C, D are anti-patterns. The trap: candidates may not know noise is a real issue or have specific tools.
questions.pattern: Noisy-label workflow: estimate noise level (audit a sample, check inter-annotator agreement). Detect suspect labels (cross-val mismatches, cleanlab). Relabel or remove. Use robust losses. Iterate. SageMaker Ground Truth supports multi-annotator consolidation. For Bedrock fine-tuning data: clean labels matter as much as quantity.

questions.scenario: A team needs to convert hundreds of TB of CSV data to a more efficient format for repeated training. They want columnar, compressed, partitioned, and Athena-queryable.
questions.question: Which storage format is OPTIMAL?
choices: Apache Parquet — columnar (read only needed columns), compressed (typically 5-10x smaller), efficient predicate pushdown, partitioned by event-date or other natural keys, supported by Athena/Spark/Pandas/SageMaker; ORC is similar — pick Parquet for AWS-default integration
Plain CSV remains optimal at scale
Excel .xlsx files
PDF documents
questions.explanation:
Apache Parquet is the de facto big-data analytical format on AWS and beyond: (1) **Columnar** — store data column-by-column, allowing analytical queries to read only the needed columns (massive I/O savings for typical analytical workloads). (2) **Compression** — Snappy/Gzip/ZSTD; typically 5-10x smaller than CSV. (3) **Predicate pushdown** — filter at the storage level, skip irrelevant row groups. (4) **Partitioning** — directory-based (`year=2024/month=05/`) for efficient slicing. (5) **Schema** — embedded, self-describing, supports complex types (nested, lists, maps). (6) Native support: Athena, Spark, Pandas/Polars, SageMaker, Glue, Redshift Spectrum. ORC has similar properties; Parquet has wider ecosystem support on AWS.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may not know Parquet's properties.
questions.pattern: Storage formats: Parquet (analytical, columnar, AWS default). ORC (Hive-native, similar to Parquet). Avro (row-oriented, good for streaming/Kafka). RecordIO/protobuf (TF/MXNet ML training). JSON Lines (debugging, simple, slow at scale). CSV (interchange, slow). Choose by access pattern: analytical/aggregations ⇒ columnar. Streaming/append ⇒ row-oriented.

questions.scenario: A team is loading data into a Redshift table for ML feature generation. Previously, INSERTs were slow; now they want to use the fastest pattern.
questions.question: Which Redshift loading pattern is OPTIMAL for large data?
choices: Use the COPY command from S3 (Parquet, Gzip-compressed CSV, Avro) — parallel load across all compute nodes, much faster than row-by-row INSERTs; or use Redshift Auto-Copy for continuous loading; for very large workloads, use Redshift Spectrum to query S3 directly without loading
INSERT one row at a time via JDBC
Email the rows to Redshift
Print to CloudWatch and then read back
questions.explanation:
Redshift COPY from S3 is the canonical bulk-load pattern: (1) Stage data in S3 (ideally Parquet, compressed CSV, or Avro). (2) `COPY tablename FROM 's3://bucket/prefix' IAM_ROLE '...' FORMAT AS PARQUET;` parallelizes the load across all compute nodes — orders of magnitude faster than row-by-row INSERTs. (3) **Auto-Copy** automates continuous ingestion as new files land. (4) **Redshift Spectrum** queries S3-resident data via Glue Data Catalog without copying — useful for huge data accessed occasionally. (5) **Streaming ingestion** — direct from Kinesis Streams/MSK to Redshift. For ML feature generation, often Redshift COPY into materialized views, then SageMaker Processing reads via the Athena/Glue + S3 path.

Options B, C, D are nonsense. The trap: candidates may default to INSERTs from familiarity with OLTP databases.
questions.pattern: Redshift loading patterns: COPY from S3 (canonical bulk). Auto-Copy (continuous). Streaming ingestion (Kinesis → Redshift). Redshift Spectrum (query without loading). For ML: Redshift ML provides in-database SageMaker integration — train and infer using SQL.

questions.scenario: A team is using AWS Glue ETL jobs that take 6+ hours each. They want to optimize cost and speed.
questions.question: Which optimization techniques apply?
choices: Right-size workers (G.1X, G.2X, G.4X based on memory needs); use Glue auto-scaling (auto-scale workers based on workload) or job bookmarks (skip already-processed partitions); push down predicates to source (Athena, Redshift); use partitioning to read only relevant data; cache intermediate DataFrames; use Glue Flex (lower cost, longer SLAs) for non-urgent jobs
Run as a single small worker only
Use Lambda for all Spark workloads
Glue cannot be optimized
questions.explanation:
AWS Glue optimization levers: (1) **Right-size workers** — G.1X (1 DPU/worker), G.2X (2 DPU), G.4X (4 DPU) for memory-heavy. Don't oversize. (2) **Auto-scaling** — Glue auto-adjusts workers based on workload. (3) **Job bookmarks** — Glue tracks processed partitions/files, skips them next run. (4) **Predicate pushdown** — filter at source via partitions/Pushdown predicates. (5) **Partitioning** — write/read partitioned data to skip irrelevant subsets. (6) **Caching** — `cache()` repeatedly-used DataFrames in memory. (7) **Glue Flex** — cheaper but variable-SLA execution, good for non-urgent batch jobs (up to 35% off). (8) **Glue 4.0+** uses optimized Spark runtime. (9) Profile via Spark UI to find bottlenecks (skewed joins, shuffle spills).

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may not know Glue Flex or job bookmarks.
questions.pattern: Glue cost/speed optimization: right-size DPU. Use Flex for non-urgent. Bookmarks to skip processed. Partition prune. Predicate pushdown. Avoid wide shuffles. Cache reused. Profile bottlenecks. For interactive development: Glue Interactive Sessions; for orchestration: Glue Workflows or SageMaker Pipelines.

questions.scenario: A team trains a 10 GB model. Inference on GPUs is fast but expensive. They want to deploy on cheaper CPU instances or even edge devices.
questions.question: Which model-compression techniques REDUCE model size and inference cost while preserving most accuracy?
choices: Quantization (FP32 → INT8/INT4 — typically 4-8x smaller, 2-4x faster, small accuracy hit), pruning (remove small-weight connections), knowledge distillation (train smaller student to mimic teacher), low-rank decomposition; SageMaker Neo automates many compilation optimizations for target hardware
Buy more GPUs
Skip inference entirely
Run only on the largest GPU instance
questions.explanation:
Model-compression techniques: (1) **Quantization** — reduce numerical precision. FP32 → INT8 typically 4x smaller, faster on hardware with INT8 support (Inferentia, modern CPUs). FP16 / BF16 for GPU. INT4 for extreme compression (some accuracy loss). Tools: PyTorch quantization, ONNX Runtime, TensorRT. (2) **Pruning** — remove low-weight connections (unstructured) or whole channels/heads (structured); requires fine-tuning post-pruning. (3) **Distillation** — train smaller 'student' model to match large 'teacher' outputs; significant size reduction with reasonable accuracy. (4) **Low-rank decomposition** — replace large weight matrices with products of smaller ones. (5) **SageMaker Neo** — compiles + optimizes models for target hardware (often combines quantization + graph optimization).

Options B, C, D miss the optimization opportunity. The trap: candidates may default to bigger hardware rather than smaller models.
questions.pattern: Compression toolkit: quantization (most common, INT8 for inference). Pruning (structured for hardware speedup). Distillation (separate smaller model). Neo compilation (target-specific optimization). Combine: quantize + distill + Neo for max savings. Always: re-evaluate accuracy after compression — small drop usually acceptable, large drop indicates over-aggressive.

questions.scenario: A team's neural network training crashes with 'CUDA out of memory.' They can't easily get a larger GPU instance.
questions.question: Which techniques REDUCE GPU memory usage during training?
choices: Reduce batch size (linear memory scaling); gradient accumulation (simulate larger batch with smaller per-step memory); mixed-precision training (FP16/BF16 — half memory + faster); gradient checkpointing (recompute forward activations during backward — trades compute for memory); smaller model or distillation; ZeRO/FSDP for distributed (shard optimizer states across GPUs)
Increase batch size to 1 million
Train without backpropagation
Disable the GPU
questions.explanation:
GPU memory savings techniques: (1) **Reduce batch size** — direct memory savings; may need higher learning rate. (2) **Gradient accumulation** — accumulate gradients over multiple micro-batches before stepping; effective larger batch with smaller per-step memory. (3) **Mixed-precision (AMP)** — FP16 or BF16 weights/activations; half memory, often faster on modern GPUs (Tensor Cores). (4) **Gradient checkpointing** — don't store all forward activations; recompute on backward pass; trades ~30% extra compute for big memory savings. (5) **Optimizer-state sharding** — ZeRO (DeepSpeed), FSDP (PyTorch); shard Adam states across GPUs in distributed training; major memory wins for large models. (6) **Smaller model or distillation**.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may not know gradient accumulation or checkpointing.
questions.pattern: OOM ladder: batch size ↓, gradient accumulation, mixed precision, gradient checkpointing, optimizer-state sharding (FSDP/DeepSpeed/SMP), CPU offloading (slow), larger instance. Modern stack: PyTorch + Accelerate or HuggingFace Transformers handles many of these via flags.

questions.scenario: A team is choosing a learning rate. The default 0.001 isn't producing good results.
questions.question: Which learning-rate practices are MOST EFFECTIVE for finding a good schedule?
choices: Use a learning-rate finder (sweep across magnitudes 1e-7 to 1, plot loss); use a warmup phase (linear ramp-up) followed by decay (cosine, exponential, step, ReduceLROnPlateau); use learning-rate scaling rules (linear scaling rule for batch-size changes); for transformers, AdamW + warmup + cosine decay is a strong default
Always use exactly 0.001 forever
Random learning rate every step
Set to 0 to be safe
questions.explanation:
Learning-rate strategies: (1) **LR finder (Smith)** — sweep LR exponentially during a short run, plot loss-vs-LR, pick LR around the steepest descent (just before divergence). (2) **Warmup** — linear ramp from 0 to base LR over first N steps; stabilizes early training, especially for transformers. (3) **Decay** — cosine annealing (smooth decrease), exponential, step (drop at milestones), ReduceLROnPlateau (drop when val loss stagnates). (4) **Scaling rules** — when scaling batch size by k, scale LR by ~k (linear scaling rule, with caveats); square-root scaling for very large batches. (5) **Optimizer-specific** — AdamW + warmup + cosine for transformers; SGD + momentum + step decay for vision CNNs.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may not realize LR is one of the most important hyperparameters.
questions.pattern: LR practices: find good LR (sweep). Warmup (early stability). Decay (late convergence). Scale with batch size. Optimizer pairs: SGD/momentum + step decay for vision; Adam/AdamW + warmup + cosine for transformers. SageMaker HPO can include LR ranges (use log-uniform).

questions.scenario: A team's gradient-boosting model (XGBoost) overfits — train AUC 0.99, val AUC 0.78. They want to regularize.
questions.question: Which XGBoost-specific regularization techniques fit?
choices: Reduce max_depth (less complex trees), increase min_child_weight (require more samples per leaf), increase gamma (minimum loss reduction to split), reduce eta/learning_rate + increase n_estimators (gradual learning), use subsample + colsample_bytree (stochastic regularization), L1 (alpha) + L2 (lambda) regularization, early stopping on validation set
Train for 100x longer
Increase max_depth to 100
Disable validation
questions.explanation:
XGBoost regularization parameters: (1) **max_depth** — lower = simpler trees (typical 3-10). (2) **min_child_weight** — minimum sum of instance Hessian per child (effectively min samples per leaf). (3) **gamma** — minimum loss reduction to make a split. (4) **eta/learning_rate** — step size; lower + more rounds = more conservative learning. (5) **subsample** — row subsampling per tree (0.5-0.9 typical). (6) **colsample_bytree** — feature subsampling per tree. (7) **alpha** (L1), **lambda** (L2) — regularization on leaf weights. (8) **early_stopping_rounds** — stop when validation metric doesn't improve for N rounds (most important practical tool). SageMaker XGBoost built-in supports all of these.

Option B (longer) makes overfit worse. Option C (deeper trees) makes overfit worse. Option D abandons honest evaluation. The trap: candidates may know generic regularization but not XGBoost-specific knobs.
questions.pattern: XGBoost overfit reduction priorities: (1) early stopping on validation. (2) Reduce max_depth. (3) Increase min_child_weight. (4) Add subsample and colsample_bytree. (5) Reduce eta + increase rounds. (6) L1/L2 regularization. (7) More data. SageMaker HPO can tune all of these.

questions.scenario: A team wants to evaluate a binary classifier comprehensively. They have prediction probabilities and ground truth.
questions.question: Which metrics provide the FULLEST picture for binary classification with possible imbalance?
choices: Confusion matrix (TP/FP/FN/TN), precision, recall (sensitivity), specificity, F1, accuracy, AUC-ROC (overall ranking quality), AUC-PR (better than ROC for severe class imbalance), calibration (predicted vs actual probability — important when probabilities matter), per-segment metrics (fairness)
Accuracy alone
Loss value alone
Latency alone
questions.explanation:
Comprehensive binary classification evaluation suite: (1) **Confusion matrix** — raw counts of TP/FP/FN/TN. (2) **Precision** = TP/(TP+FP). (3) **Recall/Sensitivity/TPR** = TP/(TP+FN). (4) **Specificity/TNR** = TN/(TN+FP). (5) **F1** = 2·P·R/(P+R) — harmonic mean. (6) **Accuracy** — only useful when classes are balanced. (7) **AUC-ROC** — ranking quality, threshold-agnostic. (8) **AUC-PR (Average Precision)** — better than AUC-ROC for severely imbalanced classes (rare positives). (9) **Calibration** — when downstream uses probabilities, check probability ↔ actual frequency match (reliability diagram). (10) **Per-segment** — break down by demographic groups for fairness (SageMaker Clarify).

Options B, C, D miss most of the picture. The trap: candidates default to accuracy without considering imbalance.
questions.pattern: Metric selection by problem: balanced binary ⇒ accuracy + AUC-ROC. Imbalanced binary ⇒ precision/recall/F1 + AUC-PR. Multi-class ⇒ macro-F1 (equal class weighting), micro-F1 (instance-weighted). Probability matters ⇒ Brier score + calibration. Cost-sensitive ⇒ expected cost. Always include confusion matrix; it disambiguates everything.

questions.scenario: A team wants to know how their CNN model uses input pixels — which regions of the image drove a particular prediction.
questions.question: Which interpretability technique fits IMAGE classification?
choices: Grad-CAM (Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping) and Integrated Gradients — produce per-image saliency maps highlighting which regions contributed to the predicted class; SageMaker Clarify also supports image explanations using SHAP-based methods
SHAP for tabular only — there's no image-specific tool
Inspect raw pixels manually for all images
Image interpretability is impossible
questions.explanation:
Vision interpretability techniques: (1) **Grad-CAM** — uses gradients of target class flowing into the last convolutional layer to produce a heatmap highlighting important image regions. (2) **Guided Backpropagation** — high-resolution per-pixel gradient. (3) **Integrated Gradients** — accumulate gradients along a path from baseline to input. (4) **LIME** — perturb image regions and observe prediction changes. (5) **SHAP** — image variants (DeepSHAP, Kernel SHAP). (6) **Attention maps** for vision transformers. SageMaker Clarify supports computer vision explanations using SHAP-based methods.

Option B is wrong — image-specific tools exist. Options C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may know SHAP for tabular but not realize image-specific techniques are mature.
questions.pattern: Interpretability by modality: tabular ⇒ SHAP, LIME, partial dependence, feature importance. Image ⇒ Grad-CAM, Integrated Gradients, SHAP image. Text ⇒ attention weights (transformers), LIME for text, SHAP for tokens. Time-series ⇒ feature importance per timestep, attention. Pair model with appropriate interpretability technique.

references.title: Computer Vision Bias Detection and Explanations with SageMaker Clarify

questions.scenario: A team needs to compare two models (A and B) on a held-out test set — they want statistical confidence that A is better than B, not just a point estimate.
questions.question: Which approach provides STATISTICAL CONFIDENCE in model comparison?
choices: Bootstrap resampling — resample test set with replacement many times, compute metric for each model on each resample, get distribution; check if metric difference is consistently positive (e.g., 95% confidence interval excludes zero); McNemar's test for paired binary classification; for production, run online A/B tests with sufficient sample size + significance test
Train accuracy alone
Pick the one with the larger model file
Whichever was trained more recently
questions.explanation:
Statistical model comparison: (1) **Bootstrap resampling** — resample test set many times (e.g., 10K), compute metric for each, build distribution of differences; if 95% CI of difference excludes 0, A is significantly better. (2) **McNemar's test** — for paired binary classification on the same test set, checks if disagreements are asymmetric. (3) **Permutation tests** — non-parametric significance. (4) **Online A/B tests** — production traffic split, measure business metric, statistical significance test (chi-squared, t-test) when enough sample size is collected. SageMaker shadow testing supports comparison without user impact. (5) **Sample size calculation** — power analysis to know how much data you need to detect a difference of size X.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may pick the model with higher mean test metric without checking if the difference is significant.
questions.pattern: Model comparison rigor: point estimate (test set metric) → confidence interval (bootstrap) → significance test (McNemar, permutation) → online validation (A/B test in production). Single test-set evaluation can mislead — the variance matters. SageMaker Experiments tracks these systematically.

questions.scenario: A team trains a 70B parameter LLM. The training job needs to checkpoint state regularly so they can recover from interruptions and analyze intermediate models.
questions.question: Which checkpointing strategy is APPROPRIATE for large-model training?
choices: Periodic checkpoints to S3 (every N steps or N hours) including model weights, optimizer state, learning-rate scheduler state, current step number; sharded checkpoints for distributed (each rank writes its shard); manage checkpoint versions (keep last K, plus best-on-validation); resume cleanly on restart; for SageMaker — SageMaker handles checkpointing for Managed Spot Training automatically
Never checkpoint — restart from scratch on failure
Save only the final model
Checkpoint only the dataset
questions.explanation:
Large-model training requires robust checkpointing: (1) **What to save** — model weights, optimizer state (Adam moments are huge — match model size in FP32), LR scheduler state, current step/epoch, RNG state for reproducibility. (2) **Cadence** — every N steps or hours; balance against I/O cost. (3) **Sharded checkpoints** — each distributed rank writes its shard; reassemble on load. (4) **Versioning** — keep latest K (rolling), best-on-validation (so you don't lose the best model). (5) **Storage** — S3 for durability + bandwidth (FSx for Lustre as cache); KMS encryption for compliance. (6) **Resume logic** — training script detects existing checkpoints and resumes from the latest. (7) **SageMaker Managed Spot Training** — handles checkpoint-resume automatically when configured. (8) **HyperPod** — auto-recovery of failed nodes mid-training.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates underestimate the operational complexity of training huge models.
questions.pattern: Large-model training operations: robust checkpointing (sharded, versioned, S3-backed). Resume on restart (idempotent training script). Failure detection (NCCL hangs, GPU errors). Auto-recovery (HyperPod, manual scripts). Monitoring (DCGM, framework metrics, loss curves). Spot tolerance — for huge jobs, prefer on-demand or capacity reservations to avoid interruption cost.

questions.scenario: A team's real-time endpoint serves a model that requires GPU. They want to use Inferentia (Inf1, Inf2, Inf3) for cost optimization.
questions.question: What is required to deploy a model on Inferentia?
choices: Compile the model with AWS Neuron SDK (for Inf2/Inf3) or AWS Neuron 1.x (for Inf1) — converts the model to a Neuron-optimized format; supported frameworks include PyTorch, TensorFlow, MXNet; SageMaker has pre-built Neuron containers; deploy on inf1/inf2/inf3 instance types; expect significant cost savings (often 30-50%+) vs equivalent GPU instances for inference
Inferentia runs unmodified GPU models with no changes
Inferentia is for training only
Inferentia is not supported in SageMaker
questions.explanation:
Inferentia is AWS's custom inference accelerator: (1) **Compile** with AWS Neuron SDK — converts your PyTorch/TF model to a Neuron-optimized format. Trn1/Trn2 are training counterparts. (2) **Frameworks** — Neuron supports PyTorch (most), TensorFlow, MXNet; many popular models (Llama, Stable Diffusion, BERT) have well-tested paths. (3) **Instance types** — inf1 (older, AWS Inferentia), inf2 (Inferentia2 — supports larger models, BF16), inf3 (Inferentia3 — latest). (4) **SageMaker integration** — pre-built Neuron containers, JumpStart includes Neuron-compiled FMs. (5) **Cost** — typically 30-70% cheaper than equivalent GPU instances for inference. (6) **Limitations** — not all model architectures are well-supported; check Neuron compatibility before committing.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may not know about Neuron compilation or assume Inferentia is GPU-compatible drop-in.
questions.pattern: Custom AWS silicon for ML: Inferentia (inf1, inf2, inf3) for inference. Trainium (trn1, trn2) for training. Require Neuron SDK compilation. Support most modern model architectures. Significant cost savings vs comparable GPUs. SageMaker JumpStart provides Neuron-ready FM deployments. For unsupported architectures, fall back to GPU (g5/p4d/p5).

questions.scenario: A team's SageMaker endpoint is hit by sudden traffic 100x normal. Autoscaling lags, requests time out.
questions.question: Which mitigations REDUCE autoscaling lag during traffic spikes?
choices: Increase MinCapacity above expected baseline; pre-warm instances before known traffic spikes; use predictive scaling (ML-based forecasting that pre-scales); use ALB target tracking with appropriate aggressive scaling thresholds; ensure container start-up is fast (small images, lazy model loading optimized); use SageMaker async inference for tolerant workloads to absorb bursts; cache predictions when possible
Disable scaling entirely
Set MinCapacity to 0 to save money during spike
Email customers to slow down
questions.explanation:
Autoscaling reaction time is the bottleneck during spikes. Mitigations: (1) **Higher MinCapacity** — keep enough capacity that small spikes don't trigger scaling at all. (2) **Pre-warming** — schedule scaling actions (CloudWatch Events / Application Auto Scaling Scheduled Actions) ahead of predictable spikes (Black Friday, news events). (3) **Predictive scaling** — uses ML to forecast load and scale ahead. (4) **Aggressive thresholds** — scale up at lower utilization (e.g., 50% instead of 70%). (5) **Fast container startup** — minimize image size, optimize model loading (parallel, lazy where appropriate). (6) **Async inference** — for non-realtime workloads, queue absorbs spikes. (7) **Caching** — for cacheable predictions (ElastiCache, CloudFront). (8) **Target Tracking + Step Scaling combo** — TT for normal, step scaling for emergencies.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may set MinCapacity = 1 and scale-up only on demand, getting timed out during real spikes.
questions.pattern: Spike-handling toolkit: MinCapacity buffer, scheduled scaling, predictive scaling, fast cold-start (small images, fast model load), async + queue, caching, multi-region. Test with load tests (Inference Recommender) to find your scaling cliff before production hits it.

questions.scenario: A team's SageMaker endpoint runs in a single Region. The team has a strict RTO of 5 minutes for recovery from regional failure.
questions.question: Which architecture meets a 5-minute RTO?
choices: Active-active multi-region — endpoints continuously running in 2+ regions, Route 53 latency-based or weighted routing with health checks for automated failover; model artifacts replicated via S3 CRR; configuration in IaC for repeatable deployment; chaos-test failover regularly to validate RTO; alternative: warm standby (smaller instances on standby, scale-up on failover, ~minutes RTO)
Single region with hourly backups — RTO measured in hours
Manual rebuild from scratch in another region
Wait for AWS to recover automatically
questions.explanation:
RTO targets and architectures: (1) **Active-active** (RTO seconds-to-minute) — endpoints running in multiple regions, Route 53 health-checked routing fails over automatically. Most expensive but lowest RTO. (2) **Warm standby** (RTO minutes) — smaller instances running in DR region, scale up on failover. (3) **Pilot light** (RTO 10s of minutes) — minimal infra (S3 artifacts, KMS keys) in DR, deploy infra on-demand. (4) **Backup & restore** (RTO hours) — restore from backups; only acceptable for non-critical. For 5-min RTO: active-active or aggressive warm standby. **Test regularly** — DR plans that aren't tested usually fail in real disasters (chaos engineering, GameDays).

Options B, C, D miss the RTO. The trap: candidates may assume 'multi-region is hard' without realizing AWS provides primitives.
questions.pattern: DR strategies (RTO/RPO trade-off vs cost): backup-and-restore (cheapest, slowest). Pilot light. Warm standby. Active-active (most expensive, fastest). Use IaC (CloudFormation, CDK, Terraform) for repeatable deployments. Replicate: S3 (CRR), KMS (multi-region keys), DynamoDB (Global Tables), ECR (cross-region replication), Model Registry (cross-region copies).

questions.scenario: A team's inference container has heavy initialization (load 5 GB model from S3, build indexes, warm caches). Cold-start adds 60 seconds.
questions.question: Which approach REDUCES cold-start latency?
choices: Pre-load model into the container image (build once, faster pull); use SageMaker startup health-check endpoints to delay traffic until warm; for serverless, use provisioned concurrency or keep instances warm via MinCapacity; for very large models, consider Inferentia/Trainium with optimized loading; use FSx for Lustre cache for repeated reads of artifacts
Cold start is unavoidable
Restart endpoint frequently
Disable model loading
questions.explanation:
Cold-start mitigation strategies: (1) **Bake model into image** — model artifacts in container image (faster than S3 download every time). Trade-off: bigger image, slower pull, but happens once per scale-up. (2) **Health checks** — SageMaker delays routing traffic to a new instance until /ping succeeds; use this to mask warm-up time. (3) **Provisioned concurrency** (serverless) — pre-warmed instances ready to serve. (4) **MinCapacity** — keep enough warm. (5) **FSx for Lustre** — fast model loading from cache (vs S3 GET). (6) **Optimized loading** — torch.compile, ONNX, lazy components, parallel loading. (7) **Smaller models** — quantization, distillation reduce loading time. (8) **Inferentia/Trainium** — specialized hardware can have fast model loading via Neuron format.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may not have measured cold-start vs warm-request latency separately.
questions.pattern: Cold-start sources: container image pull (bigger = slower). Process startup. Model load from disk/S3 to memory. Index/cache build. Network warmup (TCP, TLS). Reduce each: smaller image, faster runtime, FSx/local cache, pre-load, lazy/deferred non-essential. Health checks gate traffic until warm.

questions.scenario: A team needs to run an LLM-powered application on Bedrock with low latency (P99 < 500ms) for a customer-facing chatbot.
questions.question: Which architectural choices REDUCE P99 LATENCY for Bedrock LLM applications?
choices: Use streaming (ConverseStream) so users see tokens as they're generated instead of waiting for completion; use smaller/faster models (Claude Haiku vs Opus when capability allows); reduce input token count (concise prompts, smart RAG retrieval); reduce output token count (max_tokens); use Provisioned Throughput for guaranteed capacity; cache common responses; batch concurrent independent requests at the app layer where possible
Use the largest possible model always
Maximize input prompt length
Disable response streaming
questions.explanation:
LLM latency optimization: (1) **Streaming** — ConverseStream returns tokens as generated; perceived latency to first token is much lower than full-response. Use SSE (Server-Sent Events) on the client side. (2) **Model size** — smaller/faster models (Claude Haiku, Llama 3.1 8B) when capability suffices; reserve large models for complex tasks. (3) **Input length** — fewer input tokens → less prefill compute. Use precise prompts and RAG that retrieves only relevant chunks. (4) **Output length** — set tight max_tokens; encourage concise outputs in system prompt. (5) **Provisioned Throughput** — eliminates queuing under load. (6) **Caching** — for common queries, cache and short-circuit. Bedrock Prompt Caching (where supported) reduces cost for repeated prefixes. (7) **Region proximity** — call Bedrock in nearest region. (8) **Cross-region inference profiles** — distribute load across regions when models support it.

Options B, C, D worsen latency. The trap: candidates may ignore that streaming dramatically improves UX even if total time is the same.
questions.pattern: LLM latency stack: model size, input/output token count, streaming, hardware (Provisioned Throughput, region), caching (prompt caching, response caching). Measure: time-to-first-token (TTFT), tokens-per-second, total time. Optimize TTFT for chat UX; total time for batch.

questions.scenario: A team uses Bedrock cross-region inference profiles to handle traffic distribution across multiple regions.
questions.question: What does Bedrock CROSS-REGION INFERENCE provide?
choices: Inference profiles route requests across multiple AWS regions automatically (typically same continent), increasing throughput and availability; the request is processed in whichever region has capacity; data residency considerations apply (data may transit between regions covered by the profile)
It moves your model files to other regions permanently
It only works in one region
It requires manual region selection per request
questions.explanation:
Bedrock cross-region inference (inference profiles) routes traffic across multiple AWS regions to increase aggregate throughput and resilience. Profiles are typically defined per geography (US, EU, APAC) — your request might be served by us-east-1 or us-west-2 depending on capacity. Pros: higher throughput than any single region's quota, better availability. Cons: data residency considerations — for compliance-sensitive workloads (e.g., GDPR, FedRAMP), confirm that cross-region routing is acceptable, or use single-region. Configure via inference profile ARN in your API call.

Option B is wrong (it's request-level routing). Option C is wrong. Option D is wrong (it's automatic). The trap: data residency implications are easy to miss if not specifically considering compliance.
questions.pattern: Bedrock throughput options: on-demand (single-region quotas). Cross-region inference profiles (multi-region routing for higher throughput). Provisioned Throughput (reserved capacity, single region). For compliance-sensitive: stick to single-region; for general-purpose high-throughput: cross-region profiles.

questions.scenario: A team is rolling out a new model version. They want to validate it on real production traffic without users seeing its outputs at all (in case it's bad).
questions.question: Which deployment pattern is APPROPRIATE?
choices: Shadow testing — duplicate real production requests to the new model, compare predictions/latency offline, but only return the existing model's responses to users; SageMaker supports shadow tests on endpoints; safe way to validate new version against real traffic without risk
Replace the model in production immediately
Test only with synthetic data
Deploy to all users and roll back if problems
questions.explanation:
Shadow testing (mirror testing): real production traffic is duplicated to the new model variant. The new model's responses are LOGGED, NOT RETURNED to users — the existing model's responses still serve. Compare metrics offline: prediction agreement rate, latency, error rate, distribution of outputs. SageMaker supports shadow tests on endpoints (specify shadow variant). Zero user-facing risk; high-quality real-traffic validation. After confidence is built, switch to canary or blue/green for actual rollout.

Option B is reckless. Option C misses production-traffic shape. Option D risks bad user experience. The trap: candidates conflate canary (some users get new model) with shadow (no users get new model).
questions.pattern: Pre-rollout validation tiers: synthetic test data (offline). Shadow / mirror traffic (real traffic, no user impact). Canary (small % of users). Blue/green / linear shift (gradual all users). Choose by risk: stricter rollout for higher-stakes models. Always: define rollback alarms (latency, error rate, business KPIs), monitor live during rollout.

questions.scenario: A team's batch inference processes daily files in S3. They want event-driven processing — start the inference as soon as new data lands, not on a fixed schedule.
questions.question: Which AWS architecture is MOST APPROPRIATE?
choices: S3 → EventBridge (or S3 Event Notifications) → Lambda (or Step Functions) starts SageMaker Batch Transform job; results land back in S3, trigger downstream; alternative — S3 → SQS → fan-out to multiple consumers; for real-time-style processing per object, use SageMaker Async Inference
Poll S3 every minute with cron
Email when files appear
Manually trigger
questions.explanation:
Event-driven inference architecture: (1) S3 event notifications (or EventBridge for richer rules) fire on new object creation. (2) Target: Lambda function that calls SageMaker (CreateTransformJob for batch, or queue for async); or Step Functions for orchestration with retry/error handling. (3) Batch Transform processes the file, writes output to S3. (4) Output S3 fires next event for downstream (e.g., load to Redshift). For per-object real-time-style processing with potentially long-running models (up to 1 hour each), SageMaker Async Inference accepts requests via S3 + SQS. Eliminates polling and reduces latency-to-process.

Option B is wasteful and laggy. Options C, D don't scale. The trap: candidates may know S3 events but not connect to SageMaker job triggering.
questions.pattern: Event-driven ML patterns: S3 events → Lambda → SageMaker (batch or async). Kinesis Streams → Lambda → SageMaker (real-time). EventBridge for richer routing rules. Step Functions for multi-step orchestration with retries/conditionals. SageMaker Pipelines triggered by EventBridge for ML workflows.

questions.scenario: A team needs to deploy a Python inference handler that calls Bedrock, post-processes the response, and returns to the client. They want serverless and minimal infrastructure.
questions.question: Which deployment is MOST APPROPRIATE?
choices: AWS Lambda (Python runtime) calling Bedrock InvokeModel/Converse; API Gateway in front for HTTPS endpoint, auth, rate limiting; environment variables for config; IAM role with Bedrock invoke permission; CloudWatch Logs/Metrics for observability; consider Lambda Power Tuning for optimal memory; if response time exceeds 29s, use API Gateway WebSocket or async Lambda invoke with SQS
Run a 24x7 EC2 fleet for this lightweight service
Use a Hadoop cluster
Email Bedrock requests
questions.explanation:
Serverless Bedrock-backed APIs canonical pattern: (1) **Lambda** runs the handler — call Bedrock, post-process, return. (2) **API Gateway** (REST or HTTP) provides HTTPS endpoint, API keys/Cognito auth, rate limiting, WAF, request validation. (3) **IAM role** for Lambda with `bedrock:InvokeModel` (and optionally KnowledgeBase, Agents) permissions on specific model ARNs. (4) **Environment variables** for config (model ID, prompt templates), Secrets Manager for sensitive. (5) **CloudWatch** for logs and metrics; X-Ray for tracing. (6) **Cold-start mitigation** — provisioned concurrency for predictable latency. (7) **Long requests** — API Gateway has 29s timeout; for longer LLM completions, use WebSocket API or async Lambda invocation pattern with SQS. (8) **Cost** — Lambda is cheap when idle, scales automatically.

Options B, C, D are wrong fits. The trap: candidates may default to ECS or EC2 for any 'service' need, missing Lambda's fit for stateless API handlers.
questions.pattern: Serverless ML API stack: API Gateway → Lambda → Bedrock/SageMaker. For long-running: WebSocket / Step Functions / async Lambda. Add: WAF (security), Cognito (auth), CloudFront (caching), Secrets Manager (credentials). For agents that need state: DynamoDB session storage, ElastiCache for short-lived state.

questions.scenario: A team is operating a Bedrock-backed LLM application. They need to monitor: latency, error rates, token usage, content-policy violations, and prompt-injection attempts.
questions.question: Which observability stack APPLIES?
choices: CloudWatch metrics for latency/errors/invocations; CloudWatch Logs for application logs; Bedrock Model Invocation Logging for full prompts/responses (opt-in); Bedrock Guardrails reports flagged requests; CloudTrail for API audit; X-Ray for distributed tracing; custom CloudWatch metrics for token usage and business KPIs; alarms on cost/usage thresholds
No observability needed for managed services
Print to stdout only
Hope for the best
questions.explanation:
Bedrock observability layers: (1) **CloudWatch metrics** — Bedrock emits Invocations, InvocationLatency, InvocationClientErrors, InvocationServerErrors, plus token-count metrics; alarm on anomalies. (2) **CloudWatch Logs** — application logs from Lambda/EC2/containers calling Bedrock. (3) **Model Invocation Logging** — opt-in feature; sends full prompt + response to S3/CloudWatch Logs. Critical for prompt-injection detection, audit, and quality reviews. KMS-encrypt the destination. (4) **Bedrock Guardrails** — reports flagged content (violations), can be reviewed and trended. (5) **CloudTrail** — control-plane audit (who configured Guardrails, who called API). (6) **X-Ray** — distributed tracing if instrumented. (7) **Custom metrics** — token counts (cost tracking), business KPIs. (8) **Cost/usage alarms** — Cost Anomaly Detection on Bedrock spend.

Options B, C, D are anti-patterns. The trap: candidates may know CloudWatch generally but miss Bedrock-specific telemetry.
questions.pattern: LLM observability: standard infra metrics (latency/errors/invocations). LLM-specific: tokens in/out (cost). Quality (output ratings, agreement with reference, user feedback). Safety (Guardrail violations, suspicious patterns). Audit (full prompt/response). Cost (per-model, per-feature, per-customer). Build dashboards combining these for product, ops, and security views.

questions.scenario: A team's ML model for credit decisions is required by the EU AI Act to be classified as 'high-risk.' What governance practices APPLY?
questions.question: Which combination of governance practices is REQUIRED for high-risk AI systems under emerging AI regulations?
choices: Document data governance (training-data sources, biases, quality), risk management (identification/mitigation throughout lifecycle), technical documentation (model cards, AI service cards), record-keeping (logging requirements), transparency (inform users), human oversight (HITL), accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity, post-market monitoring, conformity assessment; tools: SageMaker Model Cards, Clarify, Model Monitor, Audit Manager, Bedrock Guardrails
No governance is needed for AI systems
Encryption alone covers all requirements
EU regulations don't apply to AWS
questions.explanation:
EU AI Act (and similar emerging regulations) impose obligations on high-risk AI systems: (1) **Data governance** — document training data, address biases, quality controls. (2) **Risk management** — systematic process across lifecycle. (3) **Technical documentation** — model cards, design rationale, evaluation results. (4) **Record-keeping** — logging requirements (audit trail of decisions). (5) **Transparency** — inform users they're interacting with AI; explain limitations. (6) **Human oversight** — humans can review, override, intervene. (7) **Accuracy + robustness + cybersecurity** — minimum performance levels, attack resistance. (8) **Post-market monitoring** — continuous performance monitoring, incident reporting. (9) **Conformity assessment** — formal compliance review before market. AWS provides tools (Clarify, Model Cards, Model Monitor, Audit Manager, Guardrails) but the customer is the AI provider/deployer responsible for compliance.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may underestimate that AI regulation is shifting from voluntary to legally mandated.
questions.pattern: AI regulation landscape: EU AI Act (risk-tiered, broadest). US — sector-specific (FTC, EEOC, FDA), executive orders. China — generative AI rules. UK — pro-innovation framework. Key controls common across: documentation, transparency, human oversight, audit, monitoring, risk management. AWS positions services to support these (Service Cards, Model Cards, Clarify, Audit Manager).

questions.scenario: A team's ML model is used to make recommendations that impact protected groups (gender, race, age). Bias audits show disparate impact: the model accepts 70% of group A but only 50% of group B for the same qualification levels.
questions.question: Which mitigation steps APPLY?
choices: Investigate root cause (biased training data, biased labels, biased features that proxy protected attributes); remediation: rebalance/augment training data, remove biased features, fairness-aware training (constrained optimization), post-processing thresholds per group (controversial), counterfactual fairness checks; SageMaker Clarify supports pre-training, post-training, and production bias audits with multiple metrics (statistical parity, equal opportunity, etc.)
Ignore the disparate impact
Drop all protected-group members
Disable the model entirely without investigation
questions.explanation:
Bias mitigation lifecycle: (1) **Detect** — SageMaker Clarify computes pre-training bias (data-only), post-training bias (model predictions), production bias (over time); metrics: Statistical Parity Difference (SPD), Disparate Impact (DI ratio), Equal Opportunity Difference (recall disparity), Predictive Parity Difference. (2) **Diagnose root cause** — biased training data sample, biased labels, biased features that proxy protected attributes (e.g., ZIP code correlates with race). (3) **Mitigate** — rebalance/augment training data for underrepresented groups, remove biased features (and their proxies), fairness-aware training algorithms (adversarial debiasing, constrained optimization), post-processing thresholds per group (controversial — may itself violate equal-treatment laws in some jurisdictions). (4) **Re-audit** — post-mitigation, verify metrics improved. (5) **Continuous monitoring** — Model Monitor tracks bias drift over time.

Option B is unethical and illegal in many jurisdictions. Options C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may know fairness concepts but not specific tools and metrics.
questions.pattern: Fairness toolkit: SageMaker Clarify (bias metrics, pre/post/production). Define protected groups, choose metrics aligned to use case (equal opportunity for credit/hiring; demographic parity for resource allocation, but caveats). Mitigate via data, model, or post-processing. Document decisions in Model Card. Monitor in production.

questions.scenario: A team's deployed model receives 1M predictions per day. They want to detect drift but receive ground-truth labels with a 30-day delay. How can they monitor in the meantime?
questions.question: Which approach is APPROPRIATE?
choices: Monitor data quality drift on inputs immediately (no labels needed) using Model Monitor's data-quality monitoring; monitor proxy signals (prediction distribution drift, confidence distribution, feature attribution drift via SageMaker Clarify); set conservative alarms; once labels arrive (30 days later), run Model Quality monitoring to confirm; cadence: daily input drift, weekly model quality (lagged)
Wait 30 days for labels before any monitoring
Skip monitoring entirely
Use random predictions as ground truth
questions.explanation:
Delayed labels are common in production ML (credit defaults, medical outcomes, conversion). Monitor what you CAN immediately: (1) **Data quality drift** (Model Monitor) — input distribution vs training baseline; no labels needed. (2) **Prediction drift** — output distribution shift over time. (3) **Confidence drift** — calibration changes (e.g., model becomes overconfident or underconfident). (4) **Feature attribution drift** (Clarify) — which features drive predictions; if it shifts, the model is using the data differently. (5) **Bias drift** — for fairness-critical models. (6) Set conservative alarms; investigate spikes immediately. (7) Once labels arrive (30 days later), run Model Quality monitoring; degradation detected at 30-day lag is still better than degradation never detected.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may believe model monitoring requires real-time labels.
questions.pattern: Monitoring without immediate labels: data quality (immediate). Prediction/confidence (immediate). Feature attribution (immediate). Bias (immediate, requires protected attribute capture). Model quality (delayed, when labels arrive). Combine: immediate alarms catch obvious issues; delayed Model Quality confirms.

questions.scenario: A team is deploying a SageMaker endpoint. They want to enforce: only data scientists with appropriate roles can deploy, all endpoints must be in a VPC with KMS encryption, and only approved Model Registry packages can deploy.
questions.question: Which AWS combination ENFORCES these controls?
choices: IAM roles with SageMaker Role Manager personas (least-privilege per role); SCPs (org-level): deny CreateEndpoint without VolumeKmsKeyId, deny without VpcConfig, deny outside specific regions; AWS Config rules check existing endpoints for compliance; Model Registry: require Approved status before deployment; CodePipeline + Lambda gate enforces approved-only deployments
Trust users to follow policies manually
Email policies once a year
Lock down nothing
questions.explanation:
Layered governance enforcement: (1) **IAM** — assign SageMaker Role Manager personas (Data Scientist, ML Engineer, MLOps). Permission boundaries cap maximum permissions. (2) **SCPs** (org-level) — deny `sagemaker:CreateEndpoint` if VolumeKmsKeyId is not set; deny if VpcConfig is missing; deny in unapproved regions via `aws:RequestedRegion` condition. SCPs are the only mechanism that no IAM permission can override — strongest enforcement. (3) **AWS Config rules** — `sagemaker-endpoint-configuration-kms-key-configured`, `sagemaker-endpoint-configuration-in-vpc`; remediate non-compliant resources. (4) **Model Registry approval** — endpoints can be deployed only from Approved model packages, enforced via CodePipeline / Lambda guard step. (5) **Audit Manager** collects evidence for compliance reviews.

Options B, C, D are anti-patterns. The trap: candidates may know IAM but not SCPs as the preventive control.
questions.pattern: Defense-in-depth ML governance: SCPs (preventive, org-wide). IAM + permission boundaries (per-role limits). Config rules (detective + remediative). CodePipeline gates (process enforcement). Model Registry approvals (workflow). Audit Manager (compliance evidence). CloudTrail (audit log). Combine multiple layers; no single control is sufficient.

references.title: AWS Config managed rules for SageMaker

questions.scenario: A team's SageMaker endpoint is invoked by a downstream microservice. The microservice should use temporary credentials, not long-lived AWS keys.
questions.question: Which AWS authentication approach is CORRECT?
choices: IAM roles for the calling service (EC2 instance profile, ECS task role, Lambda execution role, EKS service account via IRSA, Cognito for client apps); the calling service assumes the role and gets temporary credentials automatically; permissions: sagemaker:InvokeEndpoint on specific endpoint ARNs; never use long-lived access keys for service-to-service
Hardcode AWS access keys in the microservice code
Email keys to the service team
Disable authentication
questions.explanation:
Service-to-AWS authentication best practice: use IAM roles, not long-lived keys. Mechanisms: (1) **EC2** — instance profile attaches role to EC2; SDK auto-discovers via IMDSv2. (2) **ECS** — task role assigned to task definition; SDK auto-discovers. (3) **Lambda** — execution role; SDK auto-discovers. (4) **EKS** — IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA) maps Kubernetes service accounts to IAM roles; pod gets temp creds. (5) **External services** — IAM Roles Anywhere (X.509-based), or federation via SAML/OIDC. (6) **Client apps** — Cognito Identity Pools provide temp AWS creds to authenticated app users. Permissions: scope to `sagemaker:InvokeEndpoint` on specific endpoint ARNs (not wildcards).

Options B, C, D are dangerous anti-patterns. Long-lived keys leaked = compromised account. The trap: candidates know IAM users with keys but not role-based service auth.
questions.pattern: AWS auth ladder (avoid bottom): IAM roles (auto-rotated, scoped, audited). Federation (SAML/OIDC, Roles Anywhere). Cognito (client apps). Long-lived IAM user keys (LAST RESORT, must rotate). Hard-coded keys (NEVER). For machine-to-machine, always use roles.

questions.scenario: A team's training data and model artifacts are stored in S3. They need defense-in-depth protection against accidental deletion or malicious tampering.
questions.question: Which S3 features APPLY?
choices: S3 Versioning (preserves all versions of objects), MFA Delete (require MFA for permanent deletion), Object Lock with Compliance mode (write-once-read-many for regulatory retention), Cross-Region Replication for DR, bucket policy + IAM denying delete except by privileged role, lifecycle policies to S3 Glacier for long-term archive, AWS Backup for centralized backups
Trust the bucket without protections
Disable versioning to save space
Delete data periodically to keep buckets clean
questions.explanation:
S3 data-protection features: (1) **Versioning** — every PUT/DELETE creates a new version; old versions retained until explicitly deleted; recovery from accidents trivial. (2) **MFA Delete** — versions can only be permanently deleted with MFA; raises bar for malicious actors. (3) **Object Lock** — Compliance mode (no one can delete during retention period, even root); Governance mode (privileged users can override). For regulatory retention. (4) **CRR (Cross-Region Replication)** — async copy to another region for DR; SRR for same-region different bucket. (5) **Bucket policy / IAM** — deny `s3:DeleteObject` except by specific role. (6) **Lifecycle policies** — transition to Glacier Deep Archive for long-term retention at lower cost. (7) **AWS Backup** — centralized backup management with point-in-time recovery, cross-account/region.

Options B, C, D are reckless. The trap: candidates may know individual features but not the layered approach.
questions.pattern: S3 protection layers: versioning + MFA Delete (recoverable from accident/malice). Object Lock (immutable for compliance). Bucket policies + IAM (access control). Encryption (KMS at rest, TLS in transit). CRR (DR). Lifecycle / Glacier (long-term archive). AWS Backup (centralized). Block Public Access (prevent accidental exposure). For ML: training data + model artifacts both deserve all of these.

questions.scenario: A team's SageMaker training job fails intermittently with timeouts. The team wants automatic retries and notifications.
questions.question: Which mechanisms HANDLE retries and alerting?
choices: SageMaker Training Job RetryStrategy (automatic retry up to N times for transient failures); Step Functions or SageMaker Pipelines with retry logic and conditional branches; SNS topic for failure notifications; CloudWatch Events on training job state change → Lambda for custom handling; capture root cause from logs to distinguish transient (retry) vs persistent (page someone)
Hope the next manual retry works
Disable training jobs after first failure
Email the team only after a week
questions.explanation:
SageMaker training resilience: (1) **RetryStrategy** — `MaximumRetryAttempts` (1-30) automatically retries on transient internal failures (instance issues, etc.). Set on the training job config. (2) **Pipeline-level retries** — SageMaker Pipelines steps have retry policies; Step Functions has rich retry/backoff/error-handler primitives. (3) **State change events** — CloudWatch Events / EventBridge fires on training job state (Failed, Stopped, Completed); target Lambda or SNS for custom logic. (4) **SNS** — notify team on failures. (5) **Logs** — analyze CloudWatch Logs to distinguish transient (retry) vs persistent (e.g., OOM — change config) failures. (6) For Spot training — interruption is expected, checkpoint resumption handles it.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may not know SageMaker has built-in retry primitives.
questions.pattern: Resilient ML pipelines: training-job retries (RetryStrategy). Pipeline-step retries (with backoff). Event-driven alerts (EventBridge → SNS/Lambda). Distinguish transient (retry) vs persistent (alarm). Idempotent jobs (so retry is safe). Checkpointing for long jobs. Test failure modes intentionally (chaos engineering).
