questions.scenario: A team's monthly AWS bill for ML workloads has risen 40% month-over-month. They need to identify the drivers and reduce cost.
questions.question: Which AWS tools and approaches help with ML COST OPTIMIZATION?
choices: AWS Cost Explorer + Cost Anomaly Detection (identify drivers and unusual spikes); AWS Budgets with alerts; tagging all ML resources (project, team, env) for cost allocation; right-sizing tools (Compute Optimizer, Inference Recommender); use Managed Spot Training (up to 90%); SageMaker Savings Plans for committed usage; clean up idle endpoints/notebooks; multi-model endpoints to consolidate; Bedrock Provisioned Throughput vs on-demand based on volume
Increase the budget without investigation
Disable cost monitoring
Switch to a more expensive provider
questions.explanation:
ML cost optimization toolkit: (1) **Visibility** — Cost Explorer (drill-down by service/tag/account), Cost Anomaly Detection (ML-based spike detection), Budgets (alerts on thresholds). (2) **Tagging** — `Project`, `Team`, `Environment`, `CostCenter` on every resource; cost allocation reports. (3) **Right-sizing** — Compute Optimizer for general; SageMaker Inference Recommender for endpoints. (4) **Savings** — Managed Spot Training (up to 90% off training), SageMaker Savings Plans (commit to compute hours), Bedrock Provisioned Throughput (cheaper at scale). (5) **Consolidate** — Multi-Model Endpoints (many models, one endpoint), Multi-Container Endpoints (multi-framework). (6) **Cleanup** — idle endpoints (real-time endpoints bill 24/7), idle notebooks, old training artifacts in S3; lifecycle policies. (7) **Caching** — for predictable predictions, cache responses. (8) **Model compression** — smaller models = cheaper inference.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may not know specific cost levers for SageMaker/Bedrock.
questions.pattern: Cost optimization for ML: visibility (Cost Explorer + tags). Right-size (Inference Recommender, Compute Optimizer). Discount (Spot Training, Savings Plans, Provisioned Throughput). Consolidate (MME, MCE). Cleanup (idle resources). Architect (cache, compression, smaller models). Schedule (down-scale dev/test outside hours).

questions.scenario: A team needs to provide a model card for each deployed ML model, including: intended use, training-data lineage, performance metrics, fairness audits, limitations, and ethical considerations.
questions.question: Which SageMaker feature is purpose-built for this?
choices: SageMaker Model Cards — managed templates for documenting models with sections for intended use, training details, evaluation metrics, ethical considerations; integrate with Model Registry; can be exported as PDF for compliance
Email a summary to leadership
CloudWatch dashboards alone
S3 README files
questions.explanation:
SageMaker Model Cards provide a managed, structured documentation artifact for each model. Templates include: (1) **Intended use** — what the model is for, primary users, out-of-scope uses. (2) **Training details** — data sources, feature lists, model architecture. (3) **Evaluation** — metrics on test sets and per-segment. (4) **Ethical considerations** — known biases, mitigations, limitations. (5) **Approval status** — Draft / Pending / Approved / Archived. (6) Stored alongside Model Registry packages. (7) Exportable as PDF for compliance reviews. Model Cards extend the Mitchell et al. (2018) 'Model Cards for Model Reporting' standardization.

Options B, C, D don't provide structured, versioned, auditable documentation. The trap: candidates may know to document models but not the specific SageMaker feature.
questions.pattern: ML documentation hierarchy: AI Service Cards (AWS-built services like Bedrock models, Rekognition). Model Cards (your custom models). Data Cards (datasets — Datasheets for Datasets). System Cards (deployed AI systems). Tooling: SageMaker Model Cards (managed). All support audit and stakeholder communication. Update at every model version.

questions.scenario: A team needs to implement a kill switch for an autonomous AI agent that can take real-world actions (refunds, account changes). Stakeholders need confidence the agent can be stopped immediately if it misbehaves.
questions.question: Which design ENABLES a kill switch?
choices: Multi-layered: (1) feature flag/config that disables the agent's tool calls (read from DynamoDB/Parameter Store at start of each turn — flip to disable); (2) IAM policy revocation (detach the agent's role's tool-invoke permissions); (3) Bedrock Agent alias rollback (point to safe version); (4) endpoint shutdown for severe cases; (5) dashboard with one-click stop; (6) automatic kill on anomaly alarms
No kill switch is possible for AI agents
Email the agent telling it to stop
Restart the entire AWS account
questions.explanation:
Kill-switch design for AI agents: (1) **Feature flag** — config (DynamoDB, AppConfig, Parameter Store) read every turn; flip from `enabled: true` to `false` and the agent stops taking actions. Fastest. (2) **IAM revocation** — detach the agent role's permissions to invoke tools (Lambda, APIs, etc.); takes effect quickly. (3) **Bedrock Agent alias rollback** — point alias to safe (e.g., 'observation-only') version. (4) **Endpoint shutdown** — drastic, but stops all invocations. (5) **One-click admin dashboard** — surface kill controls to ops. (6) **Auto-kill** — on anomaly alarms (unusual cost, error rate, suspicious activity), automatically engage kill switch. (7) **Test regularly** — dry-run kill switches in staging; ensure they actually stop the agent. (8) **Document clearly** — runbooks, escalation, RTO for kill action.

Options B, C, D are wrong. The trap: candidates may not have thought through agent containment.
questions.pattern: Agentic AI containment: scope (least-privilege tools). Approval (HITL for high-risk actions). Logging (every action). Reversibility (undo path). Rate limits (cap blast radius). Monitoring (anomaly detection). Kill switch (multi-layer, fast, tested). Audit. For Bedrock Agents, configure Agent aliases for staged rollout/rollback.

questions.scenario: A team's SageMaker endpoint serves predictions used in clinical decision support. They need to provide explanations for individual predictions ('why was this patient flagged') to clinicians and auditors.
questions.question: Which approach provides PER-PREDICTION explanations in production?
choices: SageMaker Clarify online explainability — endpoints can return SHAP values alongside predictions, attributing the prediction to specific features; alternatively, integrate explanation generation into the inference pipeline (post-process SHAP); for tree-based models, use built-in feature importance; surface explanations in the clinical UI alongside the prediction itself
Tell clinicians to 'just trust the model'
Provide explanations only after audit complaints
Don't deploy the model at all
questions.explanation:
Production per-prediction explainability: (1) **SageMaker Clarify online explainability** — configure the endpoint to return SHAP values per prediction; clients receive both the prediction and the feature attributions in one call. Available for built-in algorithms and many custom models. (2) **Custom inference handler** — post-process predictions with SHAP/Captum/etc. to generate explanations; return both. (3) **Tree-based feature importance** — XGBoost/LightGBM expose feature contributions natively. (4) **Counterfactual explanations** — 'would have been classified as low-risk if X was different' — useful for actionable insights. (5) **UI integration** — display explanations alongside the prediction in clinician interface, with confidence and limitations. Critical for high-stakes domains where automation needs accountability.

Options B, C, D fail clinical and regulatory standards. The trap: candidates may know SHAP for offline analysis but not realize Clarify supports online explanations.
questions.pattern: Explainability deployment: training-time (offline reports). Per-prediction (online with prediction). On-demand (re-explain past prediction with stored input + model version). Audit-time (regulator asks 'why did the model say X for patient Y on date Z' — re-run with point-in-time model + Data Capture input).

questions.scenario: A team's MLOps pipeline includes training, evaluation, model registration, and deployment. Stakeholders want a clear approval gate before any model goes to production.
questions.question: Which mechanism PROVIDES a manual approval gate?
choices: SageMaker Model Registry approval status (Pending → Approved/Rejected) controls deployment; combine with CodePipeline manual approval action; Step Functions WaitForCallback / Activity tasks for human-in-the-loop steps; require approval from specific personas (model owner, governance/risk team) before deployment proceeds; full audit trail in CloudTrail
Auto-deploy every trained model
Email approval one year later
Random user approves
questions.explanation:
Approval gate patterns in MLOps: (1) **Model Registry approval** — every model package has Status (Pending / Approved / Rejected). Deployment pipeline checks status; rejects un-Approved packages. (2) **CodePipeline manual approval** — explicit 'Approve' or 'Reject' button for designated approvers; integrates with SNS for notification. (3) **Step Functions** — WaitForTaskToken / Activity tasks pause workflow until callback (e.g., from a Slack approval, a web form, etc.). (4) **Persona-specific approvers** — IAM policies specify who can flip Approval status (e.g., only senior ML engineers + governance team). (5) **Audit trail** — CloudTrail logs every approval action with identity, timestamp, justification (if added). (6) **Automated approval** for low-risk models (e.g., A/B tested with positive result, all metrics within bands).

Options B, C, D are reckless. The trap: candidates may know automated CD but miss approval gates needed for ML governance.
questions.pattern: MLOps approval flow: train → evaluate (auto: meets bar?) → register (Pending) → review (humans) → approve (Approved) → deploy (canary) → monitor → full rollout. Each step audited. Failure paths: rejected → notify model owner with reasoning; deployment failure → auto-rollback. Required for regulated industries; valuable for everyone.

questions.scenario: A team's foundation-model fine-tuning workflow uses sensitive customer data. They need to ensure: data is encrypted with customer-managed keys, training compute is isolated, and no third party (including model provider) sees the data.
questions.question: Which Bedrock + AWS configurations APPLY?
choices: Use Bedrock custom model fine-tuning (data stays in your AWS account); encrypt training data in S3 with customer-managed KMS keys (CMK); use VPC endpoints (PrivateLink) for Bedrock so traffic doesn't traverse internet; the resulting custom model is also encrypted with your KMS key; AWS / model providers do NOT train on or access your fine-tuning data; CloudTrail audits fine-tuning operations; restrict access via IAM and SCPs
Send data to the model provider via email
Disable encryption to be faster
Make all data public
questions.explanation:
Bedrock custom model fine-tuning data-protection guarantees: (1) **Data stays in your account** — fine-tuning runs in AWS infrastructure under your account; not shared with model providers. (2) **KMS encryption** — training data in S3 encrypted with customer-managed CMK; resulting fine-tuned model also encrypted with your KMS key. (3) **VPC endpoints (PrivateLink)** — Bedrock traffic doesn't traverse public internet. (4) **No-train guarantee** — AWS does not use your fine-tuning data to train its own models or share with third parties. (5) **CloudTrail** — all fine-tuning operations audited. (6) **IAM + SCPs** — restrict who can fine-tune, deploy, invoke. (7) **Compliance** — Bedrock is HIPAA-eligible (with BAA), supports SOC, ISO, PCI, FedRAMP attestations. These guarantees are the basis for enterprise Bedrock adoption.

Options B, C, D are absurd. The trap: candidates may not know specifics of Bedrock's data-protection commitments.
questions.pattern: Sensitive data + Bedrock fine-tuning checklist: KMS CMK (encrypt training data + custom model). VPC endpoints (private network). IAM + SCPs (restrict access). No-train guarantee (contractual). CloudTrail (audit). Region-pinned (data residency). HIPAA BAA + Artifact attestations as needed. Same patterns apply for SageMaker fine-tuning of open-source FMs.
